name: Trujillo and La Aldea del Obispo Historic Trail Loop
name_original: Huertas de Ánimas desde Trujillo – circular por La Aldea del Obispo – Cañada Real de La Plata
description: This moderate 25-mile touring cycling route from Trujillo explores rolling Extremadura landscapes and historical sites like the Plaza Mayor.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Trujillo offers routes through diverse landscapes, from arid coastal environments to the foothills of the Andes. The region features a mix of flat coastal roads, desert plains, and gentle inland rises, providing varied terrain for cyclists. Notable natural features include the Pacific coastline, the semi-arid landscapes with carob tree forests, and serene lagoons. The area also integrates rich pre-Columbian and colonial historical sites, making for culturally immersive rides. Best touring cycling routes around Trujillo The most popular touring cycling route is Trujillo and La Aldea del Obispo Historic Trail Loop, a 25.0 miles (40.3 km) trail that takes 2 hours 47 minutes to complete, offering a journey through historical points of interest. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Plaza Mayor of Trujillo – Trujillo Castle loop from Madroñera, a moderate 25.2 miles (40.5 km) path that explores colonial architecture and historical landmarks. Local touring cyclists also love the Encina Terrona loop from Ibahernando, a 25.9 miles (41.7 km) trail leading through rural landscapes and gentle rises, often completed in about 3 hours 40 minutes. Touring cycling around Trujillo is defined by coastal routes, arid desert environments, and historical sites like Chan Chan and the Huacas del Sol y de la Luna. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging routes. The routes in Trujillo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 9 reviews. More than 85 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Trujillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Trujillo?
A: There are over 30 touring cycling routes around Trujillo, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. These routes vary in difficulty, from moderate to more challenging options, and explore the region's unique blend of coastal, desert, and historical landscapes.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while cycling around Trujillo?
A: Cycling around Trujillo offers a fascinating mix of landscapes. You'll find routes along the Pacific coastline, through arid and semi-arid desert environments with striking contrasts of dry plains and sand dunes, and into the gentle rises of the Andean foothills. Notable natural features include the serene Conache Lagoon and the dry carob tree forests of the Cañoncillo Forest.

Q: Are there any family-friendly cycling routes in Trujillo?
A: Yes, Trujillo offers routes suitable for families, particularly those that are generally flatter and explore less challenging terrain. While specific 'family-friendly' routes are not explicitly detailed, many of the moderate routes, especially those exploring coastal areas or the outskirts of the city, can be enjoyed by families. Always check the route's difficulty and elevation profile before heading out.

Q: What historical landmarks can I visit along the touring cycling routes?
A: Trujillo is rich in historical sites that can be incorporated into your cycling tours. You can visit impressive pre-Columbian sites like the Moche pyramids of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4461605" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Huacas del Sol y de la Luna&lt;/a&gt; or the vast adobe city of Chan Chan. Within the city, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3254281" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trujillo Main Square&lt;/a&gt; and its colonial architecture are easily accessible. Consider routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1119615287/trujillo-and-la-aldea-del-obispo-historic-trail-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trujillo and La Aldea del Obispo Historic Trail Loop&lt;/a&gt; to explore historical points of interest.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Trujillo?
A: Trujillo is often called the 'City of Eternal Spring,' meaning it generally has pleasant weather year-round. However, the coastal climate can vary. The dry season (roughly May to November) typically offers cooler temperatures and less humidity, making it ideal for cycling. Always check local weather forecasts before your ride, especially for coastal winds.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Trujillo?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes around Trujillo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14706110" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Mayor of Trujillo – Trujillo Castle loop from Madroñera&lt;/a&gt;, which explores colonial architecture and historical landmarks. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527456" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Encina Terrona loop from Ibahernando&lt;/a&gt;, leading through rural landscapes.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Trujillo?
A: The touring cycling routes in Trujillo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 9 reviews. Cyclists often praise the unique combination of rich historical sites, diverse landscapes ranging from coastal to desert, and the opportunity to experience rural Peruvian life. The variety of routes, from moderate to challenging, also contributes to its appeal.

Q: Are there any routes that offer a more challenging touring cycling experience?
A: Yes, for those seeking a more challenging ride, Trujillo offers several difficult-grade routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39557028" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Herguijuela – Conquista de la Sierra loop from Conquista de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt;, which features significant elevation gains. These routes often venture into the foothills, providing more strenuous climbs and rewarding views.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by natural attractions like lagoons?
A: Absolutely. The region around Trujillo features beautiful natural attractions. For instance, the serene Conache Lagoon, just 14 kilometers from Trujillo, is a popular spot surrounded by desert sand and greenery. While not explicitly listed in the provided routes, you can find or plan routes that incorporate such natural detours. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527850" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna de las Eras loop from Botija&lt;/a&gt; is an example of a route that passes by a lagoon.

Q: Are there options for parking or public transport access to the cycling routes?
A: While specific parking details for individual trailheads are not provided, Trujillo is making efforts towards sustainable urban mobility, encouraging cycling within the city. For routes starting outside the city center, it's advisable to research local transport options or parking availability near your chosen starting point. Many routes are accessible from smaller towns or villages where parking might be easier to find.

Q: Are there any cafes or restaurants near the cycling routes for a break?
A: Yes, many cycling routes around Trujillo pass through or near towns and villages where you can find local cafes and restaurants. For routes closer to the city, you'll have ample options. For longer, more rural routes, it's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, but you can often find small eateries in the communities along the way. The region's focus on local life means you'll likely encounter places to refuel.

Q: What is the typical duration for touring cycling routes in Trujillo?
A: The touring cycling routes around Trujillo vary in length and duration. Many popular routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1119615287/trujillo-and-la-aldea-del-obispo-historic-trail-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trujillo and La Aldea del Obispo Historic Trail Loop&lt;/a&gt;, can take around 2 hours 47 minutes to complete. Other routes might range from shorter loops to longer rides of up to five hours, depending on the distance and your pace. Always check the estimated duration for your chosen route.
tips: La Aldea del Obispo is a small town surrounded by fields of cereals and oak trees. Take a look at the Iglesia Parroquial de Nuestra Señora del Rosario as you cycle through the city.
Huertas de Ánimas is a small town north of Trujillo. The town is located on an old cattle track that today is surrounded by wooden houses and oak groves.

The equestrian statue of Francisco Pizarro is located in Trujillo, a city in the Extremadura region, Spain. This imposing statue pays tribute to one of the illustrious residents of Trujillo: the conquistador Francisco Pizarro. The statue was created by American sculptor Charles Cary Rumsey (1879-1922). It is located in the Plaza Mayor of Trujillo, on a granite pedestal. Although not its original location, this statue is an important symbol in the city where Pizarro was born.

Trujillo Castle and the Alcazaba of Trujillo, two historical gems in the city of Cáceres:
Trujillo Castle (Alcazaba)
Origin: Initially built as an Arab citadel in the 9th century on Cabeza del Zorro hill.
Later renovations in the 10th and 11th centuries and Christian expansions after the Reconquista (1232).
Architecture: Irregular layout adapted to the terrain, with granite walls, barbican towers, and two cisterns.
Simple interior: parade ground, cisterns, and, later, a chapel in the keep dedicated to Our Lady of Victory.

Palace of the Marquises of Lorenzana
Origin: 16th century, Renaissance style. It was the residence of the Pizarro Hinojosa family, Lords of Torrecillas, and from 1642, Marquises of Lorenzana, a title granted to Álvaro Pérez Quiñones y Lorenzana, Governor of Panama and Guatemala.
Current use: Headquarters of the Royal Academy of Extremadura of Letters and Arts since 1980.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Huertas de Ánimas from Trujillo circular route?
faqs.answer: This touring bicycle route is predominantly composed of paved tracks, making it suitable for touring bikes. You can expect rolling hills with a mix of ascents and descents, characteristic of the Extremadura region, rather than flat terrain.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 40.3 kilometers (25 miles) long and typically takes around 2 hours and 47 minutes to complete by touring bicycle, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Komoot classifies this as a 'moderate bike ride' requiring 'good fitness.' While it's not overly steep, the rolling hills mean it's not entirely flat. Beginners with some cycling experience and good fitness should be able to manage it, especially given the paved surfaces.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a mix of historical and natural sights. You'll pass through the area of Huertas de Ánimas and a section of the Cañada Real de La Plata. Highlights include the Parish Church of Our Lady of the Rosary, La Aldea del Obispo, the Church of Huertas de Ánimas, and the historic town of Trujillo itself, with its Plaza Mayor of Trujillo, View from Trujillo Castle, and the Palace of the Marquises of Lorenzana.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails, including the Camino Mozárabe por Trujillo, Cañada Real del Puerto Miravete, and the Cordel de Aldea del Obispo. It also touches upon a variant of the Camino de Santiago, which is popular among cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: While specific details for this route are limited, the Extremadura region generally experiences hot summers. Spring and autumn are typically the most pleasant seasons for cycling, offering milder temperatures and comfortable conditions to enjoy the landscapes.

faqs.question: Is the Huertas de Ánimas from Trujillo circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a touring bicycle route predominantly on paved tracks and roads, dogs are generally permitted, especially if they are accustomed to riding in a bike trailer or basket. Always ensure your dog is safe and does not interfere with other cyclists or traffic. Leash laws should be observed when off the bike, particularly in villages or near livestock.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to cycle this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public touring bicycle route.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail, especially if starting from Trujillo?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route are not detailed, Trujillo is a tourist center. You can typically find parking in or around the town center, such as near the Plaza Mayor, to begin your circular journey. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through small cities and villages like La Aldea del Obispo and Huertas de Ánimas, as well as starting from Trujillo. You should find opportunities for refreshments in these locations. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially between villages.

faqs.question: What equipment should I bring for touring cycling in this region?
faqs.answer: For this moderate touring bicycle route, essential equipment includes a well-maintained touring bike, a helmet, appropriate cycling attire, water bottles, basic repair tools (spare tube, pump), and navigation (like the komoot app). Given the rolling hills, comfortable gearing is also recommended. Sun protection is crucial, especially during warmer months.
